notes.anchor: the tie that he was my quaestor
notes.note: The quaestor–imperator relationship was one of the most binding bonds in Roman public life: a quaestor was assigned to a senior magistrate (consul, praetor, proconsul) and acted as his financial officer and right-hand man. Roman tradition (mos maiorum) treated this bond as quasi-filial and the obligation of reciprocal support was lifelong.

notes.anchor: his brother M. Mindius (who carried on trade at Elis)
notes.note: M. Mindius was a Roman negotiator (merchant-businessman) based at Elis in the western Peloponnese, a region with active Roman commercial traffic. His death intestate or with a disputed estate created the inheritance case that Mescinius, as heir, needed Servius Sulpicius to adjudicate as proconsul of Achaea.

notes.anchor: we have procured letters to you from M. Lepidus the consul
notes.note: M. Aemilius Lepidus was consul in 46 BC (suffect from approximately May), just before his role as master of horse under Caesar. Cicero obtained supporting letters from the consul not as a directive but as a moral endorsement --- letters commendaticiae, a standard instrument of Roman patronage.

notes.anchor: you remit them, since the case is with a senator, to Rome
notes.note: Roman law gave senators a privilege of being tried or judged at Rome rather than in the provinces; Cicero invokes Mescinius's senatorial status as grounds for the provincial governor to refer contumacious disputants back to the Roman courts, strengthening Mescinius's hand in the settlement.
